questions.question: Define DDL and list three DDL commands in SQL.
keyPoints: DDL stands for Data Definition Language
Three DDL commands: CREATE (creates database objects), ALTER (modifies structure), DROP (deletes objects)

questions.question: Explain the difference between CHAR and VARCHAR data types with examples.
keyPoints: CHAR is fixed-length, VARCHAR is variable-length
CHAR(n) pads with spaces to fill n characters
VARCHAR(n) stores only actual characters up to n limit
VARCHAR is more storage-efficient

questions.question: What are the different types of constraints in SQL? Explain any three.
keyPoints: PRIMARY KEY: Unique identifier, one per table
NOT NULL: Prevents NULL values
UNIQUE: Ensures no duplicate values
FOREIGN KEY: Creates relationship between tables
CHECK: Validates values against condition
DEFAULT: Provides default value

questions.question: Write SQL to create a table called STUDENT with columns: StudentID (primary key, auto-increment), Name (not null), Age (between 18-25), Course (default 'Computer Science').
keyPoints: Use CREATE TABLE statement
Define StudentID as PRIMARY KEY with AUTO_INCREMENT
Name should have NOT NULL constraint
Age should have CHECK constraint
Course should have DEFAULT value

questions.question: What is the difference between DROP TABLE and TRUNCATE TABLE commands?
keyPoints: DROP TABLE removes entire table structure and data permanently
TRUNCATE TABLE removes all data but keeps structure
DROP cannot be rolled back easily, TRUNCATE can be
DROP frees storage space, TRUNCATE resets identity counter

questions.question: Explain the concept of referential integrity with an example.
keyPoints: Referential integrity ensures relationships between tables are consistent
Foreign key must reference existing primary key
Prevents orphaned records
Example: Order table's CustomerID must match a valid Customer in Customer table

questions.question: What is a composite key? When is it used? Provide an example.
keyPoints: Composite key is a primary key with multiple columns
Used when single column cannot uniquely identify a row
Example: Enrollments table with (StudentID, CourseID) as composite primary key

questions.question: Create a normalized database design for a Hospital Management System with tables for Patients, Doctors, and Appointments. Write CREATE TABLE statements with appropriate constraints, data types, and relationships.
keyPoints: PATIENTS table: PatientID (PK), Name, DOB, Gender, Phone, Address
DOCTORS table: DoctorID (PK), Name, Specialization, Phone, Experience
APPOINTMENTS table: AppointmentID (PK), PatientID (FK), DoctorID (FK), Date, Time, Status
Use appropriate data types (VARCHAR for names, DATE for dates, INT for IDs)
Include NOT NULL where required, DEFAULT for status
Ensure foreign key constraints for referential integrity

questions.question: Explain the differences between column-level and table-level constraints. Write SQL CREATE TABLE statements demonstrating both approaches for a library database with tables for Books and Members.
keyPoints: Column-level: Defined with column definition, applies to single column
Table-level: Defined after all columns, can apply to multiple columns
Demonstrate NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY at both levels
Books table: BookID (PK), Title, Author, Price, MemberID (FK)
Members table: MemberID (PK), Name, JoinDate, MembershipType
Use CHECK constraints at table level for validation

questions.question: Design an Employee Management System database. Create tables for Employees (with self-referencing manager relationship), Departments, and Salaries. Include all appropriate constraints and demonstrate ALTER TABLE operations.
keyPoints: DEPARTMENTS: DeptID (PK), DeptName, Location
EMPLOYEES: EmpID (PK), Name, DeptID (FK), ManagerID (FK to EMPLOYEES), HireDate, Designation
SALARIES: SalaryID (PK), EmpID (FK), SalaryAmount, EffectiveDate
Include DEFAULT, CHECK, NOT NULL constraints
Show ALTER TABLE to add/modify columns
Demonstrate referential integrity with self-join

questions.question: Write comprehensive CREATE TABLE statements for an Online Shopping System with tables for Users, Products, Orders, and OrderItems. Include all constraints, indexes, and demonstrate proper referential integrity.
keyPoints: USERS: UserID (PK), Name, Email (UNIQUE), Password, Phone, Address
PRODUCTS: ProductID (PK), Name, Description, Price (CHECK >0), Stock, Category
ORDERS: OrderID (PK), UserID (FK), OrderDate, TotalAmount, Status
ORDERITEMS: OrderItemID (PK), OrderID (FK), ProductID (FK), Quantity, PriceAtPurchase
Use appropriate data types and constraints
Include CASCADE or RESTRICT for referential actions
